coredata.dc:title: Sensitive assay of pharmaceutical amines in environmental samples and formulations by oxygenated graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Sefidan, Somayyeh Baghi
Eskandari, Habibollah
coredata.dc:description: Oxygenated graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared and used as a new colorimetric reagent for analysis of pharmaceutical amines. Citric acid was thermalized to prepare GQDs with functional groups along their edges. The prepared GQDs were used as a coupling reagent in an azo dye formation for dapsone (DAP) and metoclopramide (MET) determination. The characterization was done by scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, atomic force microscopy, X-ray diffraction spectroscopy, Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, EDX-mapping, dynamic light scattering, photoluminescence spectroscopy, Raman and FT-IR spectroscopy. DAP and MET were diazotized by nitrite to their related diazonium cations in acidic condition through a flow injection manifold. Then they were coupled to GQDs to produce the yellow colored products. Detection of the products was performed by spectrophotometry at 423 nm. Various chemical and instrumental parameters were optimized by a univariate procedure and a central composite design (CCD) chemometrics approach. The analytical method showed the linear calibrations for DAP and MET in the ranges of 0.03–20 and 0.1–40 µg mL−1, respectively. The limit of detection for both DAP and MET was of 0.01 µg mL−1, respectively. The inter-day experiments showed a satisfactory robustness of the method for determination of DAP and MET. The sample throughput found in this method was about 60 h−1. Lake, river and well water samples as well as soil samples along with some pharmaceutical formulations were analyzed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Central composite design
Pharmaceutical amines
Flow injection analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for assay of the pharmaceutical amines
Characterization of the synthesized GQDs and GODs
Optimization of chemical and instrumental parameters
Comparison with the other chemicals as coupling agents
Applicability of the method on the determination of other pharmaceutical amines

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM (I), TEM (II) and DLS (III) of the prepared GQDs.
FT-IR spectrum of: GQDs (I), GODs (II), DAP (III), DAP-GQDs product (IV), MET (V) and MET-GQDs product (VI).

The effect of nitrite (I), hydrochloric acid (II) and GQDs (III) on the sensitivity of the method. Condition: for nitrite optimization, different nitrite concentrations in 0.3 mol L−1 HCl, GQDs 1.51 mg mL−1, L1 equal to 30 cm, L2 equal to 27 cm, 250 µL of DAP 2.0 µg mL−1, 250 µL of MET 10.0 µg mL−1 and flow rate of 2.08 mL min−1. For optimization of HCl and GQDs their concentrations were varied, respectively (as mentioned in the text).

Three-dimensional (3D) response surfaces obtained based on the model for optimization of chemical variables in DAP (series I) and MET (series II). Condition: L1 equal to 79 cm, L2 equal to 27 cm, 500 µL of DAP 2.0 µg mL−1, 500 µL of MET 10.0 µg mL−1 and flow rate as 1.44 mL min−1.
The structures of DAP, MET and GQD.
The chemical pathway for DAP and MET determination.
Analytical characteristics for DAP and MET analysis.
Determination of DAP and MET in water and soil samples.
Analysis of DAP and MET in pharmaceutical preparations.
Sensitivity of the method for various pharmaceutical amines with concentration of 2.0 mg L−1.

row.entry: Relative sensitivity with respect to MET a

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The employed condition is the optimum condition of MET determination.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the DAP and MET analytical method with the other reported methods.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Linear dynamic range for DAP and MET determinations.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Limit of detection for DAP and MET determinations.

head.ce:title: Sensitive assay of pharmaceutical amines in environmental samples and formulations by oxygenated graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Basic Sciences, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, Ardabil 56199-11367, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Oxygenated graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared and used as a new colorimetric reagent for analysis of pharmaceutical amines. Citric acid was thermalized to prepare GQDs with functional groups along their edges. The prepared GQDs were used as a coupling reagent in an azo dye formation for dapsone (DAP) and metoclopramide (MET) determination. The characterization was done by scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, atomic force microscopy, X-ray diffraction spectroscopy, Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, EDX-mapping, dynamic light scattering, photoluminescence spectroscopy, Raman and FT-IR spectroscopy. DAP and MET were diazotized by nitrite to their related diazonium cations in acidic condition through a flow injection manifold. Then they were coupled to GQDs to produce the yellow colored products. Detection of the products was performed by spectrophotometry at 423 nm. Various chemical and instrumental parameters were optimized by a univariate procedure and a central composite design (CCD) chemometrics approach. The analytical method showed the linear calibrations for DAP and MET in the ranges of 0.03–20 and 0.1–40 µg mL−1, respectively. The limit of detection for both DAP and MET was of 0.01 µg mL−1, respectively. The inter-day experiments showed a satisfactory robustness of the method for determination of DAP and MET. The sample throughput found in this method was about 60 h−1. Lake, river and well water samples as well as soil samples along with some pharmaceutical formulations were analyzed.

ce:section.ce:para: Analytical azo coupling methods have been used for determination of nitrite, appropriate arylamines and phenolic compounds [1–5]. Classic quantitative azo coupling based methods use suitable and common nucleophiles as aromatic amines and phenolic compounds during coupling reactions where some of the reagents include 1-naphthylamine, N-(1-naphthyl)ethylenediamine and 1-naphthol [2,4,6–8]. Many important arylamines (such as some pharmaceuticals) can be determined by colorimetry after diazotization in the presence of excess nitrite and azo coupling reaction. The excess amounts of nitrite are used to ascertain quantitative diazotization of the analytes and it is found that the remaining nitrite has the potential to react with the coupling agents to form nitroso compounds [9,10]. The by-products are in yellow and red colors and produce a huge blank response. The blank response lowers the reproducibility and accuracy of the method and may saturate the absorbance response during colorimetry. The difficulty has been overcome by adding sulfamic acid (amidosulfonic acid) or ammonium sulfamate after diazotization (before coupling reaction) to destroy the remaining nitrite (nitrogen gas is formed) [2,4,5]. However, addition of reagents makes the procedure time and reagent consuming in addition to being environmentally unsafe. Moreover, in the related flow injection methods, an additional line is considered for adding sulfamic acid or ammonium sulfamate. As a result, the additional solution dilutes the detecting stream and decreases sensitivity of the method. Efficient coupling reagents which do not react with nitrite may be a prime aim to reach faster, safer and more efficient diazotization-coupling based determinations.

Dapsone (DAP) is an antibiotic and antibacterial non-steroidal drug that treats leprosy. Also, DAP is employed as an antiparasitic, dermatitis herpetiformis, and a commonly used medication for HIV and AIDS patients [11]. However, high doses of DAP have been proved to be connected to some undesirable effects such as the hemopoietic system disorder and cancer [12].

Metoclopramide (MET) has prokinetic and antiemetic properties in decreased gastrointestinal motility related disorders such as gastroparesis and ileus. In addition, MET prevents cancer therapy-induced emesis and acts on gastroesophageal reflux disease, dyspepsia, nausea and vomiting [13,14]. Like DAP, high doses of MET can cause movement disorders such as parkinsonism, akathisia and tardive dyskinesia [15].

In view of the pharmacological importance, appreciable methods have been prompted for DAP and MET quantification. High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [16–20], electrochemical methods [21,22], spectrofluorimetry [13] and diazo-coupling methods [23,24] have been successfully applied for determination of DAP and MET. The employed diazo-coupling reagents were dibenzoylmethane and benzoylacetone. Sulfamic acid and ammonium sulfamate were employed to remove interferences of excess nitrite prior to addition of dibenzoylmethane and benzoylacetone, respectively [23,24].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and carbon dots (CDs) are zero dimensional materials having many applications in various scientific domains. They are interesting due to their quantum confinement, optical properties, aromatic structure and edge effects [25–27]. Graphene nano-sheets and GQDs (especially when are prepared by thermalizing of citric acid) contain –COOH and –OH groups along the edges of the nano-sheets. Ideally, the nano-materials are totally conjugated (their skeleton contains carbons with SP2 hybridization) and do not contain defects such as epoxy groups, –COOH and –OH functional groups on their planes whereas graphene oxide sheets and grapheme oxide dots (GODs) contain these functional groups on their planes. The skeletal carbons of graphene oxide have SP2 and SP3 hybridization [28–30]. GODs are synthesized with different methods, and luminescence properties of GODs is tuned by controlling the ratio of the sp2 and sp3 fractions. Due to their abundant oxygen functional groups, GODs are hydrophilic and disperse well in water to form stable colloidal suspensions [30].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have found many applications in most scientific domains due to quantum confinement, aromatic structure and edge effects [31]. GQDs are generated using different carbon precursors by a variety of chemical and physical manipulations when top-down and bottom-up procedures are applied [31]. Some experimental and theoretical studies reveal the effects of different functional groups on the chemical and physical characteristics and behaviors of GQDs. Some of the moieties are OH [32,33], COOH [33,34], COO [32], NH2 [35], NO2 [36], F [37], Br [34], COCH3 [34], OCH3 [34], OCFCF2 [34], CHCF2 [34], CHCH2 [34] , Cl [38], COOCH3 [39], CONH2 [40], CON(CH3)2 [41,42], aryl groups [43], sulfur [44], folic acid [45] and valine [46]. Carbonization degree of citric acid may be controlled to prepare GQDs containing hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups. This approach is one of the most efficient and economic synthetic methods to prepare GQDs for analytical purposes [28]. Almost all of the analytical spectroscopic applications of GQDs are based on fluorescence quenching of GQDs [47]. Some of the analytical methods describe the determination of Ag(I) [44], Hg(II) [46], cyanide [48], free chlorine [49] and thiamine [50] in many samples.

Flow injection (FI)-spectrophotometric based methods may be the best approaches to solve some of the problems owing to improvement in automation, sample manipulation, reproducibility, speed of analysis, economics and safety [51].

The goal of the experimental design is to extract the maximum amount of information about the system under study in a cost-effective way [52]. In experimental design, all effective variables are changed from one experiment to another, simultaneously. The variables can influence each other and the optimum value of one might be related to the values of the others [53]. A full factorial design requires many experiments at a multilevel mode. However, central composite design (CCD) provides almost the same information as multilevel full factorial design, but with fewer experiments [54]. In CCD, it is assumed that the central point for each factor is 0 and the design is symmetrical.

Here we report on a special efficient analytical colorimetric application of GQDs. In this automatic method, DAP and MET are oxidized to their related diazonium cations by nitrite in hydrochloric acid stream prior to coupling of the formed diazonium cations with GQDs. The yellow colored products are detected by spectrophotometry. The main advantage of the diazotization-coupling method is the absence of sulfamic acid or ammonium sulfamate which can eliminate the remaining nitrite. Therefore, GQDs are added to the reaction stream immediately after diazotization step. Through this research, a CCD approach is also used to elucidate the manner in which the chemical parameters affect the response function.

Citric acid anhydrous, hydrochloric acid, acetic acid, sodium hydroxide, DAP, ethanol and sulfuric acid were obtained from (Merck, Germany). 1-Naphthol and N-(1-naphthyl)-ethylenediamine (NED) dihydrochloride were also purchased from Loba-Chemie Company (India). MET hydrochloride was obtained from Fluka. Almost all the chemicals were of analytical grade reagents.

The 1000 mg L−1 stock solution of DAP was prepared in ethanol. The MET stock solution as 1000 mg L−1 was prepared in deionized water. Working standard DAP and MET solutions were made by successive dilutions of the stock solutions.

A 0.1 mol L−1 sodium nitrite solution in deionized water (as a fresh solution) was prepared in a 100.0 mL volumetric flask. The fresh working solutions of nitrite were prepared appropriately by diluting the stock solution with deionized water.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) information was obtained by a Philips X'pert Pro X-ray diffractometer with a Cu Kα (λ = 0.1542 nm) radiation source. To study the morphologies of the prepared GQDs and graphene oxide dots (GODs) a LEO 1430VP scanning electron microscope (SEM) and a Zeiss EM10C electron transmission microscope were used. Particle size measurements were performed by employing a Horiba SZ-100z dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument. Raman spectra were also obtained by using an Almega Thermo Nicolet dispersive Raman spectrometer. All photoluminescence spectra were obtained by a Shimadzu RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained using an ENTEGRA AFM NT-MDT with silicon tips under non-contact mode.

A Perkin-Elmer Spectrum model RX-I was used for obtaining the FT-IR spectra. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) pattern and EDX maps were obtained by using a field emission-scanning electron microscopy (TESCAN- MIRAIII, Czech) at an accelerating voltage of 15 kV. UV–Vis spectra were recorded by a Shimadzu double beam spectrophotometer model UV-1650 PC equipped with a pair of matched quartz cells with 10.0 mm path length (Hellma). An oven (Beschickung-Loading Modell 100–800; Memmert) was used for thermal manipulation and materials drying.

The applied FI manifold is given in scheme S1. The FI system is composed of a three-channel peristaltic pump (Gilson, Minipuls 2) equipped with silicone rubber tubes (0.8 mm i.d.), PEEK mixing tees (VICI, USA) and 6-port-2-position low pressure injection valve (Valco, USA). The sample loop volume was 500 μL. Absorbance was measured with time by a Cecil spectrophotometer model CE 1021 equipped with a Hellma 18 µL flow-through cell (10.0 mm optical path). The working wavelength was set to 423 nm through all of the absorbance monitoring.

GQDs and GODs were synthesized by controlling the degree of citric acid carbonization. To prepare GQDs, 2.0 g citric acid was carbonized at 200 °C for 30 min. If the carbonization period had been continued for 2 h, the color would have changed from orange to black; it confirms the production of GODs. These synthesized matters were dried by freezing under vacuum condition (0.754 ± 0.021 g GQDs and 0.361 ± 0.015 g GODs). They were later dispersed separately into a sodium hydroxide solution, according to the method reported in [28]. Finally, working solutions of GQDs and GODs were prepared by appropriate dilution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for assay of the pharmaceutical amines
ce:section.ce:para: The DAP and MET contents of the samples were analyzed using a three-line FI manifold described in scheme S1. During the analysis, 500 μL of the samples were injected into the carrier stream. The procedure for treatment of water, soil and pharmaceutical samples are given in supplementary materials.
For data manipulation of the CCD experiments, a MINITAB statistical package (Minitab Inc. Release 17.0) was used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the synthesized GQDs and GODs
ce:section.ce:para: To acquire information about the size and shape of the synthesized GQDs, SEM and TEM images were obtained. SEM image display relatively regular circular GQDs with average diameters about 20 nm (Fig. 1 (I)). TEM image (Fig. 1(II)) indicates that the spherical GQDs have the average diameter about 15 nm, mostly distributed in the range of 10–25 nm. These results are in good compatibility with the previously reported data [28]. The results obtained from TEM and SEM images are also consistent with the obtained DLS data (Fig. 1(III)) which showed a narrow distribution with an average size about 12.4 nm for GQDs.

Fig. S1(I, II) shows the SEM and DLS data for GODs, respectively. SEM shows nearly spherical shape with size about 100 nm. The size is in good agreement with the DLS results (mean size equal to 83.1 nm). The corresponding AFM image of GODs (Fig. S1(III)) reveals a typical topographic height of 4–8 nm with size of about 100 nm. Consequently, considering the theoretical thickness of a graphene layer of 0.34 nm, the AFM data imply that most of GODs contain about 15 layers of graphene oxide.

The appropriate FT-IR spectra were recorded to identify the functional groups in the prepared GQDs and GODs. IR spectrum for GQDs shows the presence of some functional groups evidently (Fig. 2 (I)). The broad stretching vibrations of OH (3100–3750 cm−1), CO stretching (1696 cm−1), COO vibration (1396 (sym) and 1562 cm−1 (asym)), and C-OH groups (1120 cm−1) were attributed to GQDs. Some differences were revealed when the FT-IR spectra of GQDs and GODs (Fig. 2(I, II)) were compared. The C-OH group (stretching vibration at 1120 cm−1) in GQDs had disappeared and the C-O-C group (stretching vibration at 1016 cm−1) in GODs had appeared. The longer thermalization period in the preparation of GODs than GQDs prompted GQDs (as intermediate) to react with each other and form larger dots (GODs) [49,55].

Evaluation of Fig. 2(III, IV) shows common characteristic peaks at 832 (τCCCH), 1108 (νCC) and two peaks at 1146 and 1278 cm−1 (νOS) for DAP and its GQDs based azo product. In addition, distinct absorption bands at 3338, 3368, 3396 and 3456 cm−1 (NH stretching in NH2 groups) for DAP were disappeared in the product confirming the absence of NH2 groups in the product [56].

In addition, comparison of FT-IR spectra of MET and its product (Fig. 2(V, VI)) shows common peaks at 2800–3000, 1632, 1541,1269 and 680 cm−1 which are attributed to aliphatic CH stretching vibrations, CO stretching vibrations of amide groups, coupling of the NH bending and CN stretching vibrations, asymmetric C-O-C stretching vibration of aryl alkyl ether and CCl band [57]. Also, disappearance of the absorption bands at 3198, 3314 and 3398 cm−1 (related to NH stretching in NH2 group of MET) in the resulting product confirms the diazotization-coupling reaction [57].

The crystalline structures of GQDs and GODs were also studied by XRD technique. Two broad peaks centered at around 2θ = 23°, similar to the graphite lattice spacing, are ascribed to disordered carbon atoms in XRD pattern (Fig. S2(I)). Fig. S2(II)) shows the Raman spectra of GODs and GQDs. Raman spectroscopy is a powerful and widely used technique to characterize the grapheme like structures. The Raman spectra displays two prominent peaks of D-band and G-band at 1370 and 1605 cm−1, respectively. The D-band is related to the defects (sp3 carbons) whereas the G-band is attributed to the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbons. The intensity ratio of D-band to G-band (ID/IG) is used to define the degree of crystallinity of graphitic systems. The ID/IG ratios were found to be 2.3 and 0.81 for GODs and GQDs, respectively. The ratios indicate that GODs contain more defects than GQDs. ID/IG values of graphene based materials vary significantly depending on their preparation methods [58,59].

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of GQDs and GODs are shown in Fig. S3(I, II). The relatively excitation-independent emission spectra of GQDs means that the sp2 clusters within GQDs should be relatively uniform in both size and surface (Fig. S3(I)), whereas the PL spectra of GODs show excitation-dependent emission spectra. The maximum emission wavelength of GODs shifts from 455 to 505 nm when the excitation wavelength is increased from 320 to 420 nm (Fig. S3(II)). The results reveal that GODs are non-uniform. The results are in agreement with the results obtained for the carbon based luminescent nano-materials [60,61].

The corresponding EDX spectrum of GQD nanoparticles was obtained to assess their approximate chemical composition (Fig. S4(I)). The distribution of the elements, oxygen and carbon, in the prepared GQDs were obtained (Fig. S4(III, IV)) and carbon to oxygen atomic ratio was obtained to be 32/68. The maps demonstrate a homogeneous distribution of oxygen and carbon atoms while carbon is the minor element in GQDs.

The colorimetric determination of DAP and MET were performed by their diazotization with nitrite in hydrochloric acid medium to produce the diazonium cations of the amines, prior to coupling with GQDs.

Some batch experiments were also conducted. DAP and MET were diazotized by nitrite in hydrochloric acid. Then, the formed diazonium cations were coupled with GQDs to form the related colored products. The diazotization period for the pharmaceuticals (the time between addition of DAP and MET and GQDs) was varied and the results showed that the reactions (diazotization of DAP and MET) were very fast. The period of coupling for the diazotized DAP and MET was also varied, the resultant spectra of which are given in Fig. S5(I, II). The spectra of DAP and MET products were varied with time but with some differences. As it can be seen, for DAP a little broader and a more stable product than MET are observed. Also the DAP product spectrum shows a shoulder. Fig. S5 (insets) show the absorption spectra of the diazotized DAP and MET. A weak absorbance at 423 nm for the diazonium cations is observed. The absorbance spectra of the formed DAP-GQDs and MET-GQDs products show a huge enhancement in the sensitivity when they are compared with the spectra of the diazotized DAP and MET. In addition, the flow system in scheme S1 was applied and different concentrations of DAP and MET were injected. The absorbances resulting from the repetitive injections are given in Fig. S5(III, IV) for DAP and MET, respectively. According to the results, more sensitive detection of DAP against MET detection is apparent.

Oxidation of carbon–carbon double bonds of GQDs moiety near NN [62] or possible aggregation of the products (that may change the optical characteristics of the formed products) [63,64] may contribute in the color instability.

The instability of absorbance over time for DAP-GQDs and MET-GQDs systems may provide limitations on the reproducibility when DAP and MET are determined using batch methods. On the other hand, the fast reactivity of DAP and MET with nitrite, as well as the fast reactions of the diazotized DAP and MET with GQDs lead us to develop DAP and MET determinations by appropriate FIA approaches.

According to the principles of azo coupling reactions, an aryldiazonium cation behaves as a weak electrophile. So, the aromatic coupling agents with amino or hydroxy functional groups are favored. In Scheme 1 , a typical structure for the employed GQDs is given. Hydroxy and carboxy groups are considered as the electron donating and electron accepting groups [65], respectively. Azo coupling reaction takes place favorably in para-position than ortho-position of the aromatic coupling agents [66,67]. However, in GQDs the para-position coupling sites are absent. Therefore, the most reactive CH coupling sites on the model GQD are the nearest relative to the hydroxyl groups along the edges of GQDs.

With the same coupling agent, DAP shows higher molar absorptivity than MET in diazo-coupling reactions [24]. This observation could be related to the structure of DAP. DAP is a diamine aromatic, but MET is a mono-aminofunctionalized aromatic. Therefore, DAP is coupled with the coupling agents in the ratio of 1:2 [11]. The approximate molar absorptivities for DAP and MET products in this work were 66,170 and 28,260 L mol−1 cm−1, respectively. This may provide some hints about the stoichiometry of the coupling reactions. The reaction routes for DAP-GQDs and MET-GQDs systems are given in Scheme 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of chemical and instrumental parameters

ce:section.ce:para: The FI system in Scheme S1 was assembled for optimization and determination of DAP and MET. The concentrations of the employed chemicals, temperature and the instrumental parameters were considered for optimization. The peak of absorbance at 423 nm was measured and selected as the analytical response after injection of a known amount of DAP or MET. A univariate optimization procedure was considered and all the experiments were repeated at least in triplicate.

Initially, the chemical parameters were varied when the instrumental parameters and temperature were kept constant. Temperature, flow rate (for each channel) and sample injection volume were set as 20 °C, 2.1 mL min−1 and 250 µL, respectively. The length of reaction coils, L1 and L2 were 30 (for diazotization of DAP and MET) and 27 cm (for the coupling reactions with GQDs), respectively. The first chemical parameter, nitrite concentration, was varied and the obtained results are given in Fig. 3 (I). As it can be seen, the best sensitivity was observed for both DAP and MET at nitrite concentration of 1.0 mmol L−1 (this nitrite concentration was selected as the optimized value). Then, concentration of hydrochloric acid (in Fig. 3(II)) was varied and the obtained results showed a reduced sensitivity after increasing HCl concentration beyond 0.2 mol L−1. Also, GQDs concentration was varied and the absorbance was followed. The results in Fig. 3(III) revealed that GQDs concentration of 2.52 mg mL−1 has produced the maximum sensitivity for both DAP and MET assay. As a result, nitrite of 1.0 mmol L−1, HCl of 0.2 mol L−1 and GQDs of 2.52 mg L-1 were selected for the next studies.

The optimization process was continued by focusing on the instrumental variables. To reach the maximum absorbance in FI methods, the maximum reaction yield and the minimum dispersion must be achieved. The reaction of DAP and MET with nitrite in the presence of hydrochloric acid takes place in L1 reaction coil. Also, the length of L2 determines the efficiency of coupling reaction between GQDs and the diazotized form of the pharmaceuticals. Therefore, lengths of the reaction coils (L1, L2) were optimized, separately (Fig. S6(I, II)) and the values of L1 and L2 as 79 and 27 cm, respectively, were selected.

The role of flow rate as an important instrumental factor was also investigated in the range of 0.33–2.67 mL min−1, for each channel. Peak height increased slightly when the flow rate was varied up to 1.44 mL min−1. At higher flow rates, absorbance decreased slightly which may be due to the reduction of the period of DAP and MET diazotization and coupling reactions (Fig. S6(III)). Therefore, flow rate of 1.44 mL min−1 was selected as the optimum value.

The effect of injected volume of DAP (2.0 µg mL−1) and MET (10 µg mL−1) into the FI manifold was investigated. The injection volume was tested in the range of 20–500 µL. The sensitivity increased when sample volume was increased. Sample volume of 500 µL for both DAP and MET was selected for the next studies (Fig. S6(IV)).

Finally, the effects of temperature (in the range of 20–50 °C) and ionic strength (by adding sodium chloride in the range of 0.05–1.0 mol L−1) on the sensitivity of the DAP and MET determination methods were studied. The obtained results revealed that temperature and ionic strength had no significant effect on the sensitivity of the method. Therefore, the temperature was taken to be 20 °C for further studies.

The univariate optimization was a start point to understand the relationship between the sensitivity and the affecting parameters. The obtained results were applied to define the ranges of the chemical concentrations that must be considered by CCD optimization method.

Experimental design is a strategy to gather empirical knowledge based on the analysis of experimental data and not merely on the theoretical models. The aims of experimental design are to get more information from fewer tests and to collect the necessary information.

The CCD method, as the effective experimental design technique, was used for process analysis, modeling and optimization of the chemical variables [54] (after identification of the significant chemical variables in the univariate optimization method).

Table S1 shows the three considered factors, their coded and actual values in the CCD pattern. A fixed concentration of DAP (2.0 µg mL−1) or MET (10.0 µg mL−1) and the univariate optimization FIA parameters were applied through all of the experiments. The number of required experiments in the CCD optimization procedure with two replicates for the three affecting chemicals was 40. Tables S2 and S3 demonstrate the results of the analysis of variance (ANOVA) obtained by the CCD optimization procedure. These statistics show the relationship between the two factors and measured absorbance at the center level of the other factors. Acquired results for optimization have been shown in Fig. 4 .

The best fitted models for the measured absorbance for DAP and MET were given, respectively, as:
(1)
A = C o n s t a n t +
β 1
X 1
+
β 2
X 2
-
β 11
X
1
2
-
β 22
X
2
2
-
β 12
X 1
X 2
(2)
A = C o n s t a n t +
β 1
X 1
+
β 2
X 2
+
β 3
X 3
-
β 11
X
1
2
-
β 22
X
2
2
-
β 12
X 1
X 2
+
β 23
X 2
X 3

The positive values in the Eq. (1) show that absorbance is increasing by increasing the levels of the factors. The ANOVA results in Tables S2 and S3 demonstrate that the models are significant enough and lack of fits are justified. Eq. (1) shows (for DAP) that GQDs concentration, in the tested range, is not effective on the color development of DAP. Also, the squares of X1 and X2 and their interactions affect the absorbance with negative β coefficients. On the other hand, Eq. (2) shows (for MET) that the concentration of the reagents (HCl, nitrite and GQDs) is significant. Also, the squares of X1 and X2 and their interactions affect the absorbance with negative β coefficients, and the interaction of X2 and X3 is positively effective.

However, the CCD method proposed that optimum values of HCl, nitrite and GQDs concentrations were of 0.3 mol L−1, 1.5 mmol L−1 and 3.08 mg mL−1 (2.45 fold diluted of GQDs stock solution), respectively, for DAP assay. Also, the CCD method presents that the optimum values of HCl, nitrite and GQDs concentrations were of 0.29 mol L−1, 1.6 mmol L−1 and 3.77 mg mL−1 (2.0 fold diluted of GQDs stock solution), respectively, for MET determination method.

The results obtained by the univariate and the CCD optimization methods were compared with each other. For injection of a 2.0 mg L−1 DAP, the optimum chemical concentrations proposed by CCD method and by the univariate method were applied on the assembled FIA system, separately. The measured absorbances were 0.152 ± 0.001 and 0.141 ± 0.001 (± amounts are standard deviations) for CCD method and univariate method, respectively. Likewise, 10 mg L−1 of MET was injected when the optimum chemical concentrations proposed by CCD method and by the univariate method were applied. The measured absorbances were 0.214 ± 0.004 and 0.173 ± 0.003, respectively. The paired t-tests confirmed the presence of some meaningful differences. Therefore, the proposed chemical concentrations by the CCD method were selected for the next studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison with the other chemicals as coupling agents
ce:section.ce:para: Sensitivity of a reagent for colorimetric FI assay of an analyte depends on the molar extinction of the product and rate of the product formation. In the FIA system used for DAP and MET assay, the diazotization of DAP and MET by nitrite is very fast. Therefore, sensitivity is affected mainly by the kinetics of the coupling reactions. Under optimized conditions for DAP and MET determination in this research, different reagents were compared as the coupling agents. The results in Table S4 clearly show some facts about the established GQDs-based DAP and MET colorimetric procedures. First, GODs did not react with the diazotized DAP or MET to prepare the colored product (the result may be due to complete oxidation of carbons along the edges of GODs). Second, in the acidic stream of the FIA set up, 1-naphthol is not coupled with the diazotized MET but is coupled slowly with the diazotized DAP (GQDs and 1-naphthol have hydroxyl functional groups on their aromatic rings and may be classified as phenolic coupling agents). Finally, citric acid is not coupled with the diazotized DAP or MET.

Analytical dynamic ranges for the developed DAP and MET determination methods were obtained under their optimum conditions. The calibration parameters are given in Table 1 . The linear range for DAP and MET were 0.03–20 and 0.1–40 µg mL−1, respectively. The obtained calibration curves are given in Fig. S7.

Precision and accuracy of the DAP and MET determination methods were evaluated by injecting different concentrations of DAP and MET (Table 1) as intra-day and inter-day experiments. The obtained results show a satisfactory reproducibility and recovery for DAP and MET determinations by the presented methods. Also, the detection limit of the method for both DAP and MET determinations was found to be 0.01 μg mL−1.

The effects of various species on the determination of DAP and MET as 1.0 μg mL−1 were investigated, when the other species were added individually. The results in Table S5 show the concentrations of the added species and their effects on the recoveries of DAP and MET. The results show that the commonly added cations, anions and organic chemicals do not have considerable interferences on the DAP and MET determination whereas the tested pharmaceutical amines cause serious interferences on the determination of DAP and MET.

Three environmental water samples were gathered and analyzed by the FI methods. The water samples showed no detectable amounts of DAP and MET. Thereafter, all of the water samples spiked with two different concentrations of DAP or MET (1.0 µg mL−1 and 2.0 µg mL−1), and then were analysed (n = 7). The obtained recoveries and relative standard deviations (RSDs) were in the ranges of 96.0–106.0 and 1.0–5.2%, respectively (Table 2 ).

One soil sample was also analyzed by the established procedures. The results of the study are given in Table 2. Analysis of the soil sample showed no detectable amounts of DAP and MET. Two known amounts of the pharmaceuticals were added to the soil sample, and then were analyzed. The observed recoveries and RSDs for the experiments were in the ranges of 99.5–106.0 and 2.0–5.7%, respectively.

Also, four tablet samples containing DAP or MET were analyzed by the proposed methods. The results were listed in Table 3 and were compared with their nominal amounts of DAP or MET. The recoveries found were in the range of 97.0–100.4%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applicability of the method on the determination of other pharmaceutical amines
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimum condition employed for the determination of MET, some pharmaceutical amines were tested to define the ability of GQDs based method on the determination of the other pharmaceutical amines. The results of the study are given in Table 4 . The results show that pharmaceuticals such as sulfacetamide, sulfamethoxazole, sulfadiazine, sulfamethazine, sulfanilamide, procaine and procainamide are able to be determined by the established method with sufficient sensitivity.

The results of GQDs based DAP and MET analytical methods have been compared with some previously reported DAP and MET determination methods (Table 5 ). DAP and MET have been determined in a wider linear range than the methods reported before. In addition, the LODs obtained for DAP and MET by the reported methods [11,12,18,21,24,68–71] are higher than the LODs of the presented methods. Also, the samples analyzed by the presented methods cover wider ranges than the others. One of the major analytical advantages of the established methods against the listed ones is the speed of the analysis. The method has a flow injection basis that supports analysis of 60 samples per hour.

GQDs, a laboratory-prepared reagent, have been introduced as an efficient colorimetric reagent for analysis of pharmaceutical amines. This study was conducted using a simple assembled flow injection manifold. GQDs showed fast kinetics when coupled with the diazotized pharmaceutical amines in a hydrochloric acid stream. Since sulfamic acid or ammonium sulfamate addition is not needed to remove the excess nitrite, a more environmentally safe and faster method is introduced. Also, the additional solution (containing sulfamic acid or ammonium sulfamate) which dilutes the detecting stream (and decreases the sensitivity of the method) is absent. As far as we know, some of the most efficient coupling reagents are mainly arylamines and phenolics and are carcinogenic, potentially carcinogenic or toxic for the environment. GQDs, as defined by the presented method, establish a basis for an efficient and safe determination of the pharmaceutical amines with higher sample throughput (60 h−1).

The results of the central composite design optimization showed that concentrations of all of the chemical reagents were significant for MET. However, for DAP, hydrochloric acid and nitrite were significant. In addition, squares of nitrite and hydrochloric acid concentrations affect the absorbance with negative β coefficients for both DAP and MET.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to acknowledge financial supports of this work by the University of Mohaghegh Ardabili.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.measurement.2018.09.063.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
